name: ETH Asymmetric Trend Persistence v1
edge_mechanism: trend continuation with asymmetric exit logic where trigger, risk, and exit indicators play different roles
thesis: ETH 4h is the best similar-asset lane for testing whether continuation survives better when trigger, risk, and exit are deliberately separated instead of all using the same value reference. The edge is not a rarer entry; it is cleaner role separation in management after continuation begins.
invalidation_condition: Abort if continuation entries remain too sparse in the current tape or if asymmetric exits fail to retain more expectancy than simpler exits after costs.

pre_submit_reasoning.market_behavior_targeted: Price reclaims continuation structure, starts a follow-through leg, and then either persists enough for a structure-managed exit or stalls quickly.
pre_submit_reasoning.why_edge_should_exist: Continuation trades often fail because the same indicator is overloaded for trigger, stop, and exit. Separating roles can preserve entries while making exits respond to a different persistence signal.
pre_submit_reasoning.asset_timeframe_fit: ETH 4h is appropriate because continuation structure is visible enough to test role separation without lower-timeframe noise overwhelming exits.
pre_submit_reasoning.likely_failure_mode: If the market is too range-bound, the continuation trigger may fire but persistence exits will still give back too much before the slower exit signal responds.
pre_submit_reasoning.first_refinement_path: If entries are adequate but exit asymmetry adds no value, tighten the persistence exit before touching the trigger signal.
pre_submit_reasoning.expected_outcome_pattern: A modest set of continuation trades with occasional larger persistence winners and many first-target-dominated outcomes.
pre_submit_reasoning.what_would_count_as_success: ETH 4h plus one cross-lane show credible density and asymmetric exits improve net expectancy after costs.
pre_submit_reasoning.what_would_count_as_failure: 0 trades, sparse mirage, or persistence exits that simply give back gains.

density_sanity_check.contradictory_filters: none obvious; trigger, risk, and exit roles are intentionally separated
density_sanity_check.event_sparsity: managed by keeping entry broad and changing management rather than demanding rare perfect patterns

test_lanes.why_included: Best lane for testing asymmetric role separation on continuation management.
test_lanes.continue_if: Entries occur often enough and exit asymmetry improves expectancy after costs.
test_lanes.rotate_away_if: Continuation is too sparse or exit asymmetry adds no value.
test_lanes.expected_if_right: Continuation trades where a slower persistence exit keeps part of the move while fast exits monetize the common case.

test_lanes.why_included: Checks whether the same role-separated management survives on a higher-beta similar lane.
test_lanes.continue_if: SOL preserves the pattern with acceptable density.
test_lanes.rotate_away_if: The idea only works on ETH cleanliness.
test_lanes.expected_if_right: More explosive but noisier persistence outcomes than ETH.

test_lanes.why_included: Tests whether management asymmetry transfers to a more extreme asset.
test_lanes.continue_if: TAO still benefits from distinct trigger/risk/exit roles.
test_lanes.rotate_away_if: Volatility makes the exit asymmetry unhelpful.
test_lanes.expected_if_right: Fewer but larger persistence tails than ETH.

long: Exit remaining 30% on close through EMA_55 or 5 bars
short: Exit remaining 30% on close through EMA_55 or 5 bars
